summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – INTERIM PARENTING – Interim application for parenting orders by the husband where the 6 year old child the subject of the proceedings is an Australian citizen who has resided in Pakistan with her mother since July 2025 – Where the wife did not participate in the proceeding – Consideration of court’s power under s 111CD(1)(e) Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) including whether the subject child is habitually resident in Australia – Where it is held the court has jurisdiction but declined to make orders as sought by the husband – In the absence of the wife it is not possible to determine what orders for her future living arrangements would be in the child’s best interests – Child placed on watchlist – Interim applications dismissed

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

Williams J

Before the court for determination is the husband’s interim application for parenting orders pertaining to a child, X born 2020 (“[X]”) and an application for an anti-suit injunction.
Presently the respondent to the application, the wife and X are living in Pakistan, where it seems the wife intends to remain. The husband seeks orders to return X to Australia.
The husband has commenced proceedings in Australia, and the wife has commenced proceedings in Pakistan. The wife has not filed any documentation in the Australian proceedings, but the husband has participated in the proceedings in Pakistan, including having engaged legal representation.
Prior to making any orders in this court, it is necessary to be satisfied whether this court can or should exercise jurisdiction under the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”).
On 10 February 2026, orders were made determining the husband’s application. The husband was advised brief reasons would thereafter be delivered. These are my reasons.

BACKGROUND

Both parties are of Pakistani heritage. The husband travelled to Australia in November 2013 to pursue a masters degree at an Australian university, which he completed in December 2016.
During 2017, the husband and the wife became acquainted via their respective families. They regularly communicated and became engaged during 2017, whilst the wife remained in Pakistan. In 2018, the husband travelled to Pakistan and their ring ceremony took place in early 2018. They were married in Pakistan in early 2019, the husband returned to Australia later that month and the wife arrived in Australia in early 2019. X was born in Australia in 2020 and is a dual citizen of Australia and Pakistan.
The husband’s immigration status is unclear other than he deposes to having lodged, in 2019, a permanent residency visa application with the wife included as secondary applicant.
In October 2021, the wife and X travelled to Pakistan where they remained until February 2022, when they returned to Australia. The husband travelled to Pakistan in January 2022 and returned to Australia in February 2022 with the wife and X.
Upon the families return to Australia the husband and the wife experienced significant marital discord. In January 2024, the family again travelled to Pakistan and returned to Australia in late 2024. According to the husband, in January 2025 he advised the wife they should separate under the one roof.
In July 2025, the wife and X left Australia and commenced living in Pakistan. The husband deposes to the wife and X living at the wife’s mother’s house in City B, X attending a local private school and to communication difficulties with both the wife and X since their arrival in City B.
In October 2025, the husband filed divorce proceedings in Pakistan and the divorce has now been granted. Despite the divorce application being filed by the husband, he appears disconcerted by the speedy resolution of the divorce. In October 2025, the wife filed a Petition under ss 7 and 10 Guardian and Wards Act, 1890 in the Civil and Family Court at City B, seeking appointment as X’s guardian.[1]
Although the husband says he has not participated in the guardianship proceedings in Pakistan because he believes this court has jurisdiction, as deposed at paragraph 37 of his affidavit filed 29 January 2026, the husband has engaged legal representation in Pakistan on several occasions in that court.[2]
On 26 November 2025, the husband filed an Initiating Application in this court seeking interim parenting orders and various restraints and on 29 January 2026 he filed an Application in a Proceeding seeking to restrain the wife from continuing with the City B guardianship proceedings on an ex parte basis.
Because the wife has not engaged in the proceedings in this court, it is not clear whether she raises any objection to the exercise of jurisdiction.
Both applications were listed for hearing on 10 February 2026 and determined on that day.

DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON BY THE HUSBAND

The husband relied upon the following documents:
Initiating Application filed 26 November 2025
Application in a Proceeding filed 29 January 2026
His affidavit filed 29 January 2026
Affidavit of service of Kafish Sultan filed 28 and 29 January 2026 and 9 February 2026
Outline of Case filed 9 February 2026.
There were no documents filed by the wife and she did not participate in the hearing.

PRELIMINARY ISSUE OF SERVICE ON THE WIFE

Because the wife did not file any documents and did not attend the hearing, I queried whether she had been served with the husband’s documents and was aware of the hearing date.
As to service of the Initiating Application on the wife, the husband relied on the affidavit of his solicitor, Kafish Sultan filed 28 January 2026. Mr Sultan deposes to forwarding the documents to lawyers in Pakistan via email on 8 January 2026 together with a letter requesting personal service on the wife at two possible addresses. At paragraph 11, Mr Sultan deposes to being advised by the Pakastani lawyers service had been effected by delivery of the documents to a domestic servant, Ms C, at the wife’s home who confirmed the wife and her family lived at the address. Further, at the time of service the wife appeared on the first-floor balcony and was identified by the process server as the wife, because he had a photograph of her which was extracted from the Pakastani proceedings. The affidavit of service viewed by Mr Sultan states the event was recorded on a mobile phone and the affidavit of service he relies upon was properly sworn and notarised.
In addition to the service at the wife’s home, Mr Sultan deposes to forwarding the documents via email to the wife at her usual email address, on 28 January 2026.
The email contained information, including the dial-in details for the first hearing before a Registrar on 29 January 2026. Mr Sultan further deposes the wife’s email address was extrapolated from email chains provided to him by the husband wherein the husband and wife had previously corresponded.
The husband relied on a further affidavit of his solicitor, filed 9 February 2026 in relation to service of the Application in a Proceeding.
Annexure 2 to that affidavit is a notarised affidavit of Mr D, who deposes to being a legal professional and an associate at the law firm representing the husband in Pakistan. The notarised affidavit concerns service of the husband’s applications on the wife, including attempts to personally serve her.
At paragraph 5 of his affidavit, Mr D deposes to contacting the wife’s lawyer in the Pakastani proceeding, on 7 February 2026, to enquire if the lawyer would accept service of the Application in a Proceeding, which he did not. Because the lawyer refused to accept service, Mr D refers to subsequent actions he took to attempt to serve the wife personal. At paragraph 10 he deposes to leaving the documents next to the main door of the wife’s residence on 8 February 2026.
During submissions I was advised the husband’s solicitor had forwarded to the wife’s email an email from my chambers advising the listing date of 10 February 2026. Paragraphs 7, 8 and 9 of the affidavit of the husband’s solicitor filed 9 February 2026 refer to advising the wife of the hearing date.
Even if I accept the documents are likely to have been brought to the attention of the wife, the hearing in Australia took place one clear day after service. I consider that timeframe would not enable the wife to obtain legal representation in Australia. I am unable to conclude the wife was accorded adequate notice of the Application in a Proceeding and except for an order placing the child on the watchlist, orders were made dismissing all interim applications.

LEGAL PRINCIPLES

Australia is a signatory to both the 1980 Convention on the Civil Aspects of International Child Abduction (“the 1980 Abduction Convention”) and the 1996 Convention on Jurisdiction, Applicable Law, Recognition and Co-Operation in Respect of Parental Responsibility and Measures for Protection of Children (“the 1996 Protection Convention”). Pakistan is not a party or signatory to either convention and the husband is therefore unable to seek return of X to Australia via a Central Authority and nor is there any process for reciprocity or enforcement of Australian orders in Pakistan.
The application of s 111CC and s 111CD of the Act under Pt X111AA, Div 4, Subdiv B of the Act, depends upon consideration of whether a court in a non-convention state jurisdiction has to take measures directed to the protection of children.
Relevantly s 111CC provides:
Application of this Subdivision

This Subdivision applies only if an issue under this Act is whether a court, as opposed to any of the following authorities, has jurisdiction to take measures directed to the protection of the person of a child:

(a) a central authority or competent authority of a Convention country;

(b) a competent authority of a non‑Convention country.

As per Austin J in Bajek & Bajek [2024] FedCFamC1F 466 (“Bajek”) at [27]), the effect of s 111CC is to ensure the provisions within Subdivision B apply only in the event of jurisdictional conflict between an Australian Court and a “competent authority” in another country. The courts of Pakistan, which is a non-convention country, are defined under s 111CA(1), as being “competent authorities”.
To make the required factual finding, either expert evidence or a document tendered in conformity with s 174 of the Evidence Act 1995(Cth) (“the Evidence Act “) is required (per Austin J in Bajek at [15]).
Subject to s 111CC and pursuant to s 111CD of the Act, this court may exercise jurisdiction to make a Commonwealth personal protection measure. It is uncontentious the parenting orders the husband seeks fall within the definition of a Commonwealth personal protection measure (Part XIIIAA, Division 4, Subdivision A of the Act).
Under s 111CD(1)(e), jurisdiction may be exercised if X is habitually resident in Australia and meets the requirements of s 69E (1) of the Act. In this case, because X is an Australian citizen s 69E(1)(b) is satisfied and the outstanding jurisdictional issue is the state of X’s habitual residence.

Pakistani jurisdiction

First, I now turn to consider whether the Pakastani court has jurisdiction to take measures directed to protecting the person of a child.
There was no expert evidence adduced by the husband. Rather, counsel for the husband sought to rely upon the wife’s application for guardianship orders pertaining to X, and affidavits in support, which are annexed to the husband’s affidavit filed 26 November 2025 and which was tendered as Exhibit A-2. Further, the case print out from the Pakastani case file pertaining to the wife’s guardianship application was tendered as Exhibit A-3. The case print out refers to past hearings in the Pakastani proceedings and the anticipated hearing in February 2026 and details the legal representation of both parties in that proceeding. I was also advised the instructor for counsel for the husband could forward an electronic copy of the relevant Pakistani statute.
Although the documents comprising Exhibits A-2 and A-3 do not satisfy the requirements of the Evidence Act, an electronic copy of the statute would likely suffice.
In the specific circumstances of this case, I accept there are legitimate guardianship proceedings in Pakistan which have been instituted by the wife which would enable the court to take measures directed to the protection of the person of a child. That much may be readily inferred from the Prayer for relief sought by the wife in the guardianship proceedings dated October 2025[3] and by the husband’s participation in the proceeding, albeit limited to objecting to the Pakistani court exercising jurisdiction, rather than the Australian court.

Habitual residence

Secondly, turning now to X’s habitual residence.
The husband bears the burden of proving to the court X is habitually resident in Australia.

Relevant legal principles of habitual residence

The question of whether a person is habitually resident in a particular country is a question of fact, not an artificial legal construct, and requires an evaluation of all relevant circumstances.
The law in relation to habitual residence in Australia is well settled. The seminal authority is the High Court decision of LK v Director-General, Department of Community Services [2009] HCA 9; (2009) 237 CLR 582 (“LK”) in which French CJ, Gummow, Hayne, Heydon and Kiefel JJ said:
... First, application of the expression "habitual residence" permits consideration of a wide variety of circumstances that bear upon where a person is said to reside and whether that residence is to be described as habitual. Secondly, the past and present intentions of the person under consideration will often bear upon the significance that is to be attached to particular circumstances like the duration of a person's connections with a particular place of residence.
...
... it may be accepted that "[h]abitual residence, consistent with the purpose of its use, identifies the centre of a person's personal and family life as disclosed by the facts of the individual's activities". Accordingly, it is unlikely, although it is not necessary to exclude the possibility, that a person will be found to be habitually resident in more than one place at the one time. But even if place of habitual residence is necessarily singular, that does not entail that a person must always be so connected with one place that it is to be identified as that person's place of habitual residence. So, for example, a person may abandon a place as the place of that person's habitual residence without at once becoming habitually resident in some other place; a person may lead such a nomadic life as not to have a place of habitual residence.
...
When speaking of the habitual residence of a child it will usually be very important to examine where the person or persons who are caring for the child live – where those persons have their habitual residence. The younger the child, the less sensible it is to speak of the place of habitual residence of the child as distinct from the place of habitual residence of the person or persons upon whom the child is immediately dependent for care and housing. But if, as the writings about the Abduction Convention and like instruments repeatedly urge, the question of habitual residence of a child is one of fact, it is important not to elevate the observation that a child looks to others for care and housing to some principle of law like the (former) law of dependent domicile of a married woman.
... examination of a person's intentions will usually be relevant to a consideration of where that person habitually resides. Sometimes, intention will be very important in answering that question. The example of a person who leaves a jurisdiction intending not to return is one such case. But unlike domicile, considerations relevant to deciding where a person is habitually resident are not necessarily confined to physical presence and intention, and intention is not to be given controlling weight.
...
... when considering where a child is habitually resident, attention cannot be confined to the intentions of the parent who in fact has the day-to-day care of the child. It will usually be necessary to consider what each parent intends for the child. When parents are living together, young children will have the same habitual residence as their parents. No less importantly, it may be accepted that the general rule is that neither parent can unilaterally change that place of habitual residence. The assent of the other parent (or a court order) would be necessary. But again, if it becomes necessary to examine the intentions of the parents, the possibility of ambiguity or uncertainty on the part of one or both of them must be acknowledged.
It follows ... that to seek to identify a set list of criteria that bear upon where a child is habitually resident, or to attempt to organise the list of possible matters that might bear upon the question according to some predetermined hierarchy of importance, would deny the simple observation that the question of habitual residence will fall for decision in a very wide range of circumstances. And examination of decided cases in the area does not require the identification of a closed set of criteria, or the attribution of predetermined weighting between them.

...
Moreover, the approach described in [Punter] accords with the general tenor of decisions in the United States of America. It may be observed of those decisions that there is seen to have been a division between the Circuit Courts of Appeals about the relevance of the parents' subjective intentions for the child or children concerned. When it is also observed, however, that the resolution of the competing approaches has been to invite attention to whether presence at a place has a "degree of settled purpose from the child's perspective" (emphasis added), the difference in expression of the relevant considerations may not be great. At all events, a thread common to the leading decisions in the United States remains the need to look at all of the circumstances of the case. And it is that approach, as described in Punter, which should be followed.
Additionally, to find someone is habitually resident in a place, they must generally have lived there for an appreciable period: Zotkiewicz & Commissioner of Police (No.2) [2011] FamCAFC 147; [2011] (2011) FLC 93-472 ("Zotkiewicz”).
In Zotkiewicz at [82]–[83], the Full Court of the Family Court stated the task of a judge was twofold:
In our view, the observations made by Beaumont and McEleavy sit very comfortably with what the High Court has said most recently in LK. Accordingly, we consider the task of the Judge was twofold. The first was to ascertain whether the parents had a shared intention that the child would live in Poland with a sufficient degree of continuity that their purpose could properly be described as settled. The second was to determine whether the period of time spent in Poland was sufficiently appreciable for it to be said that the underlying reality of the connection between the child and Poland was such as to justify a finding he was habitually resident in that country.
In approaching this task, the Judge was obliged to construe the Regulations having regard to the principles and objects of the Convention, recognising “that the appropriate forum for resolving disputes relating to a child’s care, welfare and development is ordinarily the child’s country of habitual residence”.
Counsel for the husband relied upon the following facts and circumstances to determine X’s habitual residence as at the date of the application:
X was born in Australia and is an Australian citizen;
Prior to July 2025, apart from the two occasions of travel to Pakistan referred to above, she has lived here all her life in Australia;
X was well integrated into her social community in this country; and
X was connected to her life in Australia, including receiving medical treatment and attending kindergarten and school.
As to parental intention, I accept both parents intended X to be habitually resident in Australia prior to her removal to Pakistan. Thereafter, the husband’s intention was and is for the child to be habitually resident in Australia.
I accept that prior to July 2025, it probably was the wife’s intention for the child to remain in and be habitually resident in Australia. The wife’s intention after July 2025 is more ambiguous. The communications between the parties annexed to the husband’s affidavits demonstrates the negotiations between them and the uncertainty of the child’s future proposed living arrangements after the wife’s departure for Pakistan. I am able to infer by the wife’s application to the Pakistani court, which includes reference to living in Pakistan and the possibility of living in another country, that her intention as at October 2025, was most likely not to be habitually resident in Australia and to live in Pakistan or some other country.
Having regard to the statements of the High Court in LK at [34], the child’s habitual residence need not be the same as the wife’s habitual residence, even though they are living in the same country. The wife’s intentions about her habitual residence may not accord with the parent’s respective intentions as to the child’s habitual residence. Parental intention is but one of a range of factors to be considered and it is accepted the general rule is neither parent can unilaterally change the place of habitual residence, absent the assent of the other parent or court order (LK at [34]).
In this case, X is an Australian citizen and except for the last six months or so has lived in Australia, although she has travelled to Pakistan on two occasions. The husband agreed to X travelling to Pakistan in July 2025, but according to him, not for her to remain living in that country. He commenced proceedings in this court in November 2025, presumably when it became apparent to him the wife had no intention of returning with the child to permanently live in Australia, wherein he sought the return of the child to Australia and for her to be placed in his care. I accept that since July 2025 the child would have been integrated into her life in Pakistan in her mother’s care, attending school, socialising and spending time with her extended maternal family. However, the child’s experiences in Pakistan since then do not displace her long-standing connection with and habitual residence in Australia.
I find X is habitually resident in Australia.

Should the court exercise power?

Because X is an Australian citizen and I have found she is habitually resident in Australia, the two preconditions to exercise power under s 111CD(1)(e) have been satisfied. The outstanding issue is whether the discretion conferred by the Act (s 111CD(1)) should be exercised by the court.
In Pascarl & Oxley [2013] FamCAFC 47; (2013) FLC 93-536 the Full Court of the Family Court held at [85] – [86], that the power extends to cases where the subject children are a not in Australia.
As stated by Austin J in Bajek at [59]:
The relevant test then for the exercise of jurisdiction and power is what is in the best interests of the child. The determination of their best interests in accordance with the paramountcy principle (s60CA and s65 AA) requires advertence to s60CC of the Act. .......
The husband sought a raft of orders including the return of X to Australia and she primarily live with him.
When asked whether there would be any utility in making such orders because the child was presently living in Pakistan, counsel for the husband was unable to provide any assistance, other than she submitted findings about the child’s habitual residence may be helpful in the Pakistani proceedings.
There was no expert evidence about how a court in Pakistan would address and determine parenting disputes, what weight would be accorded to findings or orders of this court or indeed whether there was any prospect of enforcement of Australian orders in Pakistan.
Absent expert evidence about matters in the preceding paragraph and evidence from the wife about the relevant statutory considerations, particularly as she alleges in the Pakistani proceeding that the father has subjected her to family violence and coercive behaviour, which allegations the father denies, it is impossible to determine what orders would be in X’s best interests. I therefore do not intend to exercise my discretion to make parenting orders about the child’s living arrangements and the time she should spend with each parent.
The only order which I intend to make is to place the child on the watchlist, so that if the wife returns to Australia with the child, and the husband intends to pursue his application for parenting orders in this country, the child will remain in Australia pending agreement between the parties or court order.
I will make the relevant order, dismiss the Applications in a Proceeding and list the Initiating Application for mention on a future date to enable the husband to consider whether he intends to pursue his application for final parenting orders in this court.
